# Adgent — full content (llms-full.txt)

> The AI analyst for performance marketers. Adgent is an AI media buyer that reads Meta, Google and TikTok accounts as one, reasons about account structure, delivers a verdict-first daily brief on the Aware -> Ask -> Apply loop, and executes fixes on the advertiser's approval. Read-only by default; never an autonomous spender.

Full readable text of every page on adgent.app, for AI answer engines. Turkish versions live under /tr/.


========================================================================
URL: https://adgent.app/
========================================================================

 Aware Ask Apply 
 
# The senior analyst you can talk to .

 Adgent watches your Meta, Google and TikTok accounts as one brain, tells you what's working and what's bleeding — and applies the fix from the same chat, when you say go. 
 A senior media buyer's judgment, 24/7, at a fraction of the salary. 
 Read-only by default · you approve every change 

 ROAS 3.82× ▲ 
 Meta · Google · TikTok synced 
 CPA $22.10 ▼ 
 
 Adgent · live on your account 
 
 Why did Meta ROAS drop yesterday? 

 Spend held flat, but CBO_CORE_US pushed 38% of budget to Audience Network at 1.1× while feed sits at 4.2× . That's the drop. You told me last month to protect feed ROAS above 3× — so the exclusion I've prepped keeps it there. 
 
 Apply exclusion 
 Nothing changes until you approve 

 Build a live placement dashboard 
 Set an agent to watch this daily 
 Analyze this campaign's creatives 

 Ask anything — or say “launch a new campaign across channels” 

 Just ask — in plain language 

 Why did Meta ROAS drop yesterday? 
 Audit my placements 
 Which ad set has the best CPA? 
 Build me a weekly report 
 Build a dashboard for this client 
 Is my retargeting working? 
 Where am I wasting spend? 
 Compare Meta, Google & TikTok 
 Launch a new campaign across channels 

 Scale my best campaign 20% 
 What changed since last week? 
 Find audience overlap 
 Why is CPM rising? 
 Draft a prospecting campaign 
 Which creatives are fatiguing? 
 Break down my top creative's DNA 
 Where do viewers drop off in this video? 
 Pause what's underperforming 

 Intelligence + memory 
 
## It remembers your brand's golden rules .

 Every analyst you've ever hired had to be told your rules twice. Adgent learns them once — from what you say in chat and the calls you make — and never forgets. Its judgment compounds on your account. 

- Learned, not configured. Golden rules form from your prompts and your approve/reject decisions — no setup screen. 
 
- Carried across every session. The context accumulates. Tomorrow's brief already knows what you told it last month. 
 
- Yours, and cross-platform. Your rules and account memory follow the brand across Meta, Google and TikTok — not locked to one platform. 

 Adgent · learning In the background · nothing to manage 

 You, in chat “Don't scale anything past €500/day without flagging me — we've been burned by fast ramps.” 
 Adgent will remember this on every campaign 

 You, on a suggestion Rejected “merge prospecting + retargeting” — again . 
 Noted — it won't suggest merging these again 

 You, in chat “Judge everything against Shopify revenue , not platform ROAS.” 
 Reports now reconcile to your store first 

 You never set this up. Adgent learns from how you talk to it — and doesn't forget. 

 The 3A loop 
 
## Aware. Ask. Apply. One loop.

 Three layers of an analyst's job, in one product — awareness to know, conversation to dig, execution to act. Control stays with you the whole way. 

 01 · Aware 
 
### Intelligence

 Adgent watches every account overnight and writes the verdict before you're awake. 

- A daily brief ranked by impact
 
- A single health score per account
 
- Anomaly detection across Meta, Google & TikTok

 02 · Ask 
 
### Conversation

 Ask anything, at any level — and get an analyst's answer, not a chart to interpret. 

- Question any ad, campaign or account 
 
- Placement & demographic audits on demand
 
- Generate a custom report from one message

 03 · Apply 
 
### Execution

 When you're ready, act from the same chat. Read-only by default; you approve every move. 

- One-tap fixes applied from chat
 
- Build cross-channel campaigns by message
 
- Every change waits for your approval 

 Real output 
 
## Not a promise. An actual report .

 A real account audit Adgent ran for a hotel chain (anonymized). One pass surfaced roughly 31,400 TL/month in recoverable spend — and flagged a number most tools would have reported blindly. 

 Account audit · a hotel chain · Meta · Google · TikTok 
 4 findings, ranked by recoverable spend 
 
 ~31,400 TL / month potential 

 Optimizing toward a value-less event Trust Gate 
 The account optimizes for a Purchase event that carries no value. ROAS here is unmeasurable — most tools report a number anyway. Adgent flags it instead of inventing confidence. 

 Zero retargeting in 90 days Opportunity 
 No RTG campaign is live. Site visitors and add-to-carts are never re-engaged — the cheapest demand in the account is going unworked. 
 ▲ est. +12,800 TL / mo 

 Two prospecting ad sets overlap 41% High 
 LAL_2% and INT_TRAVEL bid against each other on the same users, inflating CPM on both. 
 ▲ est. +9,200 TL / mo 

 Placement leak to Audience Network High 
 38% of spend lands on Audience Network at a fraction of feed performance. 
 ▲ est. +9,400 TL / mo 

 Every figure links back to where to verify it in Ads Manager. Adgent decides — you confirm.

 Built from a sentence 
 
## Ask for it in chat. Adgent builds it.

 A dashboard, a monitoring agent, a restructure plan, a new campaign — you describe it in plain language and Adgent assembles it from your live account data. Then you approve. 

 Build me a reservations dashboard, last 28 days 
 Assembled from Meta · Google · TikTok + Shopify 

 Reservations 4.8K ▲ 12.4% 
 ROAS 3.82× ▲ 8.1% 
 CPA ₺22.10 ▼ 6.0% 

 Dashboard Live · auto-refreshing 

 Watch my placements and flag leaks daily 
 Agent live — scanning every morning 

 Critical 
 CBO_CORE_US Leaking to Audience Network — exclude 
 ₺1,743 est./mo 

 Watch 
 RETARGET_7D Frequency climbing — creative fatigue 
 ₺620 est./mo 

 Monitoring agent Runs daily · you approve 

 Plan a restructure to fix the iOS leak 
 4 steps · sequenced to protect learning 

 1 Exclude Audience Network on CBO_CORE_US now 
 2 Shift freed budget to Feed, +20% now 
 3 Hold — let the ad set re-stabilize 48h 
 4 Refresh retargeting creative day 3 

 Restructure plan Nothing runs until you say go 

 Build a prospecting campaign for the spring launch 
 Drafted across Meta & Google — ready to review 

 Spring · Prospecting · Meta 3 ad sets · ₺8,000/mo Ready 
 Spring · Search · Google 12 keywords · ₺5,000/mo Ready 
 Spring · Retargeting awaiting audience size Review 

 New campaign Drafted · launches on approval 

 Why chat 
 
## A dashboard can't answer “ why ”.

 Everyone else hands you a board to decode. Adgent hands you a conversation: ask the question, get the answer, then apply it from the same place. Even your reports are one message away. 

- “Why is CPA up this week?” — answered with evidence
 
- “Where do viewers drop off in this ad?” — scene by scene
 
- “Build me a dashboard for this client” — laid out for you
 
- “Build me a weekly report” — generated, ready to send

 Adgent · custom report 
 
 Build me a weekly report for the client. 
 
 Done — Meta, Google & TikTok, last 7 days. Spend 48,210 TL , blended ROAS 3.8× cross-checked against Shopify revenue, two flags worth a look. Want it as a PDF or right here? 
 
 Export PDF 
 Generated from live data 

 Trust Gate · attribution maturity 
 We won't quote a number we can't stand behind. 

 ROAS unmeasurable on this campaign 
 Optimizing toward a value-less event. We say so — we don't print a confident multiple. 

 Inside the 7-day window — still attributing 
 This number is a draft and may revise up. Flagged, never trusted blindly. 

 Trust Gate 
 
## The only tool that tells you when the number is lying .

 Most platforms report ROAS even when it can't be measured. Adgent doesn't. If a campaign optimizes toward a value-less event, or a metric is still inside the attribution window, we flag it — and refuse to manufacture false confidence. Honest beats impressive. 

 Creative intelligence 
 
## It reads every creative like a strategist — not a tagger.

 Adgent pulls your live ads automatically — no upload step — and reads each one the way a senior buyer would. It names the creative's DNA, walks the story scene by scene, and shows you exactly where viewers drop off. Then it tells you which creatives are fatiguing and what to do — as an action you approve. Analysis, never generation. 

### Creative DNA

 Every ad, decoded: hook, format, persona, angle, offer, visuals and audio — so you can see why a creative works, not just that it did. 

### Scene-by-scene storyboard

 Video ads broken down shot by shot — what happens in each moment, so a weak opening or a slow middle is obvious at a glance. 

### Where viewers leave

 A creative funnel that tracks the hold-curve across your ads — the exact scene where attention drops, so you fix the leak instead of guessing. 

### Fatigue, verdict-first

 It flags the creatives wearing out — before the numbers slide — and hands you a dollar-valued refresh you can apply from chat. 

 Getting started 
 
## Connect your channels once. Then just talk.

 STEP 01 

### Connect your accounts

 Authorize Meta, Google and TikTok — plus GA4, Shopify and Search Console for context. Read-only by default; nothing moves without your word. 

 STEP 02 

### Adgent learns your accounts

 It builds baselines and audits every channel overnight — pacing, fatigue, placement drift, attribution maturity. 

 STEP 03 

### Aware, ask, apply

 Read the morning brief, ask whatever you need, and apply fixes or build campaigns from chat — on your approval. 

 Why Adgent 
 
## Analyze or act — everything else makes you choose.

 Reporting tools go deep but never touch the account. Platform AIs and rule engines act — but with no judgment, and never on your side. Adgent is the only one that does all five at once, and remembers your brand while it does. 

 Capability 
 Reporting tools 
 Platform AIs 
 Automation rules 
 Adgent 

 Executes across platforms — ✓ ✓ ✓ 
 Reasons about account structure — — — ✓ 
 Deep config & measurement diagnostics ✓ — — ✓ 
 Chat-native verdicts + live reports — — — ✓ 
 Remembers your brand's rules — — — ✓ 

 One column checked all the way down. That column is the whole point. 

 The math 
 
## It pays for itself in the first audit.

 The question isn't whether Adgent is worth it. It's how much you're leaving on the table without it. 

 Questions 
 
## Good questions, answered plainly.

Q: Does Adgent change my campaigns on its own? 
 No. Access is read-only by default. When a fix or a campaign is ready, Adgent prepares it and waits — you apply it from chat with one message. The control is always yours; nothing is automatic. 

Q: Which ad platforms does it cover? 
 Meta, Google and TikTok Ads, read and reasoned about as one account — and it pulls in GA4, Shopify and Search Console so reports reflect real revenue, not just platform-reported numbers. 

Q: What can I actually do from chat? 
 Ask about any ad, campaign, or account; run placement and demographic audits; generate custom reports; apply prepared fixes; and build cross-channel campaigns — all by message, all on your approval. 

Q: What is the Trust Gate? 
 It's how Adgent stays honest about numbers. If a campaign optimizes toward a value-less event, or a metric is still inside Meta's 7-day attribution window, we flag it rather than report a confident figure. We'd rather tell you a number can't be trusted than pretend it can. 

Q: Will it really pay for itself? 
 In our audits, a single account's first pass typically surfaces meaningful recoverable spend — before counting the analyst hours saved. Talk to sales and we'll show you what Adgent finds in your account. 

Q: Who is Adgent for? 
 Agencies managing many accounts, ecommerce brands running their own spend, in-house growth teams, and independent performance marketers — anyone who'd rather talk to an analyst than decode a dashboard.

# Built around your accounts.

 Every plan covers Meta, Google and TikTok — and reads GA4, Shopify and Search Console for context. Tell us what you're running and we'll put together the right fit. 
 Talk to sales 

 What you get 
 
## Every plan covers every channel.

 Aware 
 Intelligence and answers across every channel. 

- Meta, Google & TikTok in one view
 
- Daily brief & health score
 
- Anomaly detection
 
- Trust Gate flagging
 
- GA4, Shopify & Search Console context

 Ask 
 Answers about your account, by message. 

- Ask-anything chat 
 
- Placement & demographic audits
 
- Custom reports on demand
 
- Client-ready exports
 
- Slack & email delivery

 Execution — always on your approval. 

- One-tap fixes from chat
 
- Cross-channel campaign builds
 
- Read-only by default
 
- Roles & permissions
 
- Multi-account & agency structure

 Questions 
 
## Plainly.

Q: Do all plans really include every channel? Yes. Meta, Google and TikTok are included on every tier — there's no "channel add-on". The difference between plans is execution and scale, not which channels you can see. 
 
Q: How is it priced? Pricing depends on how many ad accounts you connect, how many seats you need, and whether you want execution as well as intelligence. Tell us what you're running and we'll walk you through the options. 
 
Q: What counts as an ad account? One connected Meta, Google or TikTok ad account equals one. A brand running multiple channels uses one slot per account; agencies connect as many as they manage. 
 
Q: What data does it read beyond ads? Alongside Meta, Google and TikTok, Adgent reads GA4, Shopify and Search Console — so reports reflect real revenue and on-site behavior, not just platform-reported numbers. 
 
Q: Does Adgent change my campaigns on its own? No. Access is read-only by default. When a fix or a campaign is ready, Adgent prepares it and waits — you apply it from chat. The control is always yours; nothing is automatic.

 About 
 
# We're building the analyst , not the dashboard.

 Performance marketers drown in data and starve for judgment. Adgent exists to flip that — to do the thinking, and hand back a verdict you can act on before your coffee's cold. 

 The market has enough tools that show you numbers. It has almost nothing that tells you what they mean. 
 Every performance team runs the same ritual. Open Ads Manager. Scan the campaigns. Try to remember what was normal last week. Hunt for the one thing that's quietly bleeding money. It's skilled work, done by hand, every single morning. 
 We think that work should be done for you — not with another chart, but with a conclusion. Adgent reads your Meta, Google and TikTok accounts as one, overnight — pulling in GA4, Shopify and Search Console for context — and writes the brief a senior analyst would leave on your desk: what changed, why it matters, and what to do about it. Ranked by impact. Evidence attached. 
 Then it goes one step further than a report can: when you're ready, you apply the fix — or build the next campaign — from the same chat. Read-only by default, always on your approval. Adgent decides; you confirm. 
 That's the whole product philosophy. Lead with the verdict. Show the evidence underneath. Act in place when asked. Say nothing you didn't need to hear — and never quote a number we can't stand behind. 

 How we build 
 
## Three principles, held quietly.

 01 · CONSIDERED 
 
### Every number earns its place

 If it doesn't change a decision, it doesn't make the brief. Each figure carries a unit and a reason for being there. 

 02 · CONFIDENT 
 
### The conclusion comes first

 We did the analysis, so we lead with it. The evidence sits underneath for when you want to check our work. 

 03 · QUIET 
 
### Calm beats loud

 No alarms, no badges screaming for attention. Severity means severity. The interface gets out of your way. 

 Who it's for 
 
## Built for the people running the spend.

### Agencies

 Triage many client accounts from one ranked brief instead of opening a dozen tabs. 

### Ecommerce brands

 Catch placement drift and fatigue before they eat into margin on your own spend. 

### Growth teams

 Give the whole team one source of truth on what changed and what to do next. 

### Independents

 The senior analyst you can't afford to hire, watching your accounts every night. 

 2026 Founded to make judgment, not dashboards, the default. 
 3 Ad channels read as one brain — Meta, Google and TikTok. 
 6 Sources read together — ads, GA4, Shopify and Search Console. 
 Remote A small team of marketers and engineers. 

 Request a demo 
 
## Put an analyst on your account .

 Connect your accounts and Adgent gets to work across every channel. We listen closely to the teams we onboard. 

- Connects Meta, Google & TikTok
 
- Direct line to the team
 
- Shape the roadmap

 Product · 7 min read · July 2026 
 
# What is an AI media buyer?

 The term is new, the job isn't. An AI media buyer reads your ad accounts like a senior strategist, tells you what to do and why, and makes the change on your approval — not a dashboard with a chatbot bolted on. 

 N 
 
 Nurettin Demiral 
 Notes from building the analyst 

 Short answer 
 An AI media buyer is software that reads your ad accounts like a senior strategist — reasoning about account structure, pacing, creative fatigue and attribution — then tells you what to do and why, and executes the change on your approval. Unlike a dashboard, it commits to a conclusion; unlike platform automation, it works for the advertiser, not the auction. The best ones stay read-only by default and are never autonomous spenders. 

 Search "AI media buyer" and you'll find two kinds of answer: hype that promises to "replace your team," and tools that are really just dashboards with a chat box. Neither describes the job. So here's a plain-language definition, and an honest map of what the category actually contains. 

## The short definition

 An AI media buyer is software that does the reasoning part of a media buyer's job. It reads your ad accounts across platforms, reasons about how they're built — budget structure, placements, audiences, creative, attribution — and commits to a conclusion about what to change and why. Then it prepares the change and executes it when you approve. 

 The word that matters is reasoning . A media buyer's value was never pulling the numbers; it was knowing which number matters today, why it moved, and what to do about it. That judgment is the thing an AI media buyer automates — the click at the end is the easy part. 

 A dashboard hands you data. An AI media buyer hands you a decision. 

## What it is not

 The category gets muddy because three very different things get called "AI for ads." They're worth separating: 

- Not a reporting dashboard. A dashboard renders your data beautifully and leaves the analysis to you. The decision is still yours to make, alone, at 8am.
 
- Not platform automation. Meta Advantage+ and Google Performance Max are the platforms' own AI — and they optimize toward the platform's auction, not your margin. An AI media buyer sits on your side of the table.
 
- Not a rules engine. "If CPA > X, pause" is automation without judgment. It fires on a threshold; it can't tell you why CPA moved or whether pausing is the right call.

## AI media buyer vs. dashboard vs. platform AI vs. rules

 The fastest way to place the category is side by side. Here's how an AI media buyer differs from the three things it gets confused with, across the capabilities that actually matter: 

 Capability Reporting dashboard Platform AI (Advantage+, PMax) Rules engine AI media buyer 

 Reasons about why a metric moved — — — ✓ 
 Reads across Meta, Google & TikTok as one partial — — ✓ 
 Works for the advertiser, not the auction ✓ — ✓ ✓ 
 Cross-checks against real revenue partial — — ✓ 
 Executes the fix — ✓ ✓ ✓ 
 Waits for your approval before writing n/a — — ✓ 
 Explains its confidence and flags bad data — — — ✓ 

 The column on the right is the whole reason the category exists: judgment and execution, on the advertiser's side, with the human still holding the last click. 

## How the category got here: manual → rules → platform AI → AI media buyer

 The job didn't appear overnight. It's the fourth step in a twenty-year arc, and each step automated a different layer while leaving the hardest one — judgment — to a human: 

- Manual buying. A person set every bid, budget and audience by hand. Total control, zero scale.
 
- Rules & scripts. "If CPA > X, pause." Scale arrived, but the rules were blind to context — they fired on a threshold, never on a reason.
 
- Platform AI. Advantage+ and Performance Max automated bidding and placement — but optimized toward the platform's auction, and hid the account structure from the buyer.
 
- AI media buyer. The first layer that automates the reasoning — reads the whole account, commits to a conclusion, explains it, and executes on approval. It works for you, not the auction.

## What a real one does, day to day

 Strip away the marketing and the job breaks into one loop with three moves — Aware, Ask, Apply . A capable AI media buyer runs all three: 

### Aware — it watches, and writes the verdict first

 Overnight it reads every account across Meta, Google and TikTok as one book, builds baselines, and detects what changed. In the morning you get a brief ranked by impact, not a wall of charts. This is the same principle behind why we lead with the verdict : conclusion first, evidence underneath. 

### Ask — it answers, in plain language

 You ask "why did Meta ROAS drop yesterday?" and get a strategist's answer with evidence, not a chart to decode. That includes the awkward truths a dashboard glosses over — like a blended ROAS hiding a placement leak , or a number that's still inside the attribution window and shouldn't be trusted yet. 

### Apply — it acts, on your approval

 When a fix is ready, it's a prepared action you approve with one message. Nothing writes to a live account on its own. Read-only by default is the whole point: the judgment is automated, the control stays with you. 

## Why the honest version matters

 The reason to be careful about the definition is trust. An AI media buyer touches real client money, so the version worth using is the one that knows the limits of its own numbers — it flags a metric it can't stand behind instead of printing a confident figure. "Impressive but wrong" is worse than useless when there's spend on the line. 

## Should an AI media buyer be fully autonomous?

 Most of the category markets on autonomy — "set it and forget it," "replaces your team," "cuts management time 80%." It's the wrong promise, and it's worth saying plainly why. 

 Advertising isn't a closed game with a fixed reward. Attribution windows revise numbers for a week; a conversion event can be mis-configured so ROAS is literally unmeasurable; a promising placement can be view-through credit in disguise. A system that acts autonomously on those numbers doesn't remove risk — it removes the human who would have caught the bad number. The judgment should be automated. The last click should not. 

 So the better design is read-only by default with approval-gated execution : the AI does the analysis, ranks by impact, states its confidence, prepares the change — and waits. You get the speed of automation and keep the accountability. That's the difference between an AI media buyer you can put on a client account and one you can only demo. 

## How to choose an AI media buyer

 If you're evaluating tools in this category, five questions separate the real ones from the dashboards-with-a-chatbot: 

- Does it reason, or just report? Ask it "why did this move?" — a real one gives a cause, not a chart.
 
- Is it read-only by default? Anything that writes to a live account without your approval is a liability on client money.
 
- Does it cross-check against real revenue? Platform-reported ROAS alone isn't ground truth — it should reconcile to GA4, Shopify or your store.
 
- Does it flag what it can't measure? The honest ones say "this number can't be trusted yet" instead of inventing confidence.
 
- Does it work across channels as one account? Meta, Google and TikTok read together, not three separate tools bolted side by side.

 That's the bar we build Adgent to: a senior analyst's judgment across Meta, Google and TikTok, honest about what it can and can't measure, executing only on your word. If you want to see what it would say about a real account, request a demo — fifteen minutes, connected read-only. 

## Frequently asked

Q: What is an AI media buyer, in one sentence? 
 Software that reads your ad accounts like a senior strategist, tells you what to do and why, and executes the change on your approval — reasoning about account structure, not just rendering metrics. 

Q: How is it different from a dashboard? 
 A dashboard renders your data and hands the analysis back to you. An AI media buyer does the analysis: it ranks issues by impact, says how confident it is and why, and proposes the specific fix — then applies it when you approve. 

Q: Is it the same as Meta Advantage+ or Performance Max? 
 No. Those are the platform's own automation, optimizing toward the platform's auction. An AI media buyer sits on the advertiser's side, reasons across Meta, Google and TikTok together, and cross-checks platform numbers against real revenue. 

Q: Does it change campaigns automatically? 
 It shouldn't. A well-built AI media buyer is read-only by default: it prepares each change as an approval-gated action and only writes to a live account once you say go. 

Q: Does an AI media buyer replace a media buyer? 
 No — it changes what the buyer spends time on. It automates the reasoning and the busywork (nightly account reads, anomaly detection, report generation) so the human focuses on strategy, creative direction, and the final call on every change. The best setups keep a human in the loop, not out of it. 

Q: How is an AI media buyer different from programmatic advertising? 
 Programmatic automates the buying of impressions in real time within an ad exchange. An AI media buyer sits a layer above: it reasons about your whole account across platforms, decides what should change and why, and executes on your approval. Programmatic is a buying mechanism; an AI media buyer is a decision-maker. 

Q: What channels does an AI media buyer cover? 
 The useful ones read Meta, Google and TikTok as a single account, and pull in context sources like GA4, Shopify and Search Console so reports reflect real revenue rather than platform-reported numbers alone. 

Q: Is an AI media buyer safe to use on client accounts? 
 It is when it's read-only by default and every write is approval-gated, audited and reversible. Access model matters more than raw capability: a tool that can act autonomously on client spend is a risk; one that prepares changes and waits for your click is not. 

 Keep reading

 Attribution Placement drift: the leak in your average

 Product · 8 min read · July 2026 
 
# AI media buyer vs Performance Max vs dashboard: which do you need?

 They get compared as if they're the same purchase — but they're not even the same category. One reports what happened, one optimizes the auction for you, one reasons about your account for you. Here's how to tell which one you actually need. 

 N 
 
 Nurettin Demiral 
 Notes from building the analyst 

 Short answer 
 Pick by the job. A dashboard if you just need to see the numbers and read them yourself. Performance Max if you want Google's AI to auto-optimize bids and placements inside its own auction. An AI media buyer if you want judgment on your side — reasoning across Meta, Google and TikTok, cross-checked against real revenue, executing only on your approval. Most serious advertisers run all three. 

 Ask an AI engine "AI media buyer vs Performance Max vs dashboard" and it'll try to rank them like competing products. That framing is the mistake. These three are compared constantly, but they aren't rival tools fighting for the same slot — they're three different layers of the same job, and the honest answer to "which do you need" is usually "it depends what you're trying to do, and probably more than one." 

 So let's separate them cleanly. A dashboard reports. Performance Max optimizes the auction. An AI media buyer reasons for you. Get that distinction right and the choice makes itself. 

 One shows you the score. One plays the game. One tells you whether you're playing the right game at all. 

## The three, side by side

 The fastest way to place them is against the capabilities that actually decide the outcome. Filled honestly — Performance Max is genuinely good at what it does, and the table says so: 

 Capability Dashboard Performance Max (platform AI) AI media buyer 

 Whose side it's on yours (reporting) the auction yours 
 Reasons about why a metric moved — — ✓ 
 Cross-platform (Meta + Google + TikTok) partial Google only ✓ 
 Executes changes — ✓ ✓ 
 Waits for your approval n/a — ✓ 
 Cross-checks vs real revenue partial — ✓ 
 Flags untrustworthy numbers — — ✓ 
 Best for seeing the data auction-side scale judgment + execution 

 Notice the table doesn't strawman Performance Max — it executes, it scales, it's genuinely strong inside Google's auction. The gaps are structural: it works for the auction, on one platform, and it can't tell you why . 

## When a dashboard is enough

 A dashboard — Looker Studio, a GA4 view, whatever your agency built in a spreadsheet — does one thing well: it renders your data so you can read it. And for a real set of situations, that's genuinely all you need. 

 If you run a single channel, you check it yourself every day, and you already know what each metric means and what you'd do about it — a clean dashboard is the right tool. You don't need software to reason for you if you're doing the reasoning fine on your own. The dashboard's honesty is its limit: it hands you the numbers and stops. The analysis, the "why," and the decision are all still yours. 

 Where a dashboard runs out of road is the moment you have more accounts than mornings. Two platforms, five clients, a ROAS that dropped and you don't know why — a dashboard shows you that it dropped and leaves you to work out the rest. That's not a flaw; it's just where its job ends. 

## When Performance Max is the right tool

 Performance Max is Google's goal-based campaign type: you give it a target and creative assets, and its AI decides bids, placements and audiences across Search, Display, YouTube, Gmail and Maps to hit that target. Meta's equivalent is Advantage+. And here's the honest part — they're good at what they do. 

 Fed clean conversion data and a real optimization goal, Performance Max is a strong auction-side engine. It reacts to bid signals faster than any human, tests placement combinations at a scale you couldn't manage by hand, and genuinely finds pockets of cheap conversion volume. If your job is "get me more of this conversion at roughly this cost inside Google," Performance Max often does it well. 

 Its limits are structural, not quality. It optimizes toward Google's auction, not your margin — so it's happy to spend into view-through credit or double-count conversions your store never saw. It's Google-only, so it can't reason about how Meta and TikTok interact with it. And it hides account structure, so when it drifts you often can't see why. Performance Max is a superb executor. It was never designed to be the judgment on your side of the table. 

## When you need an AI media buyer

 An AI media buyer is the layer none of the others cover: it does the reasoning part of a media buyer's job. It reads your accounts across Meta, Google and TikTok as one book, reasons about how they're built and why a number moved, commits to a conclusion, and prepares the change — then executes it when you approve. 

 You need one when the question you keep asking is "why," not "what." Why did ROAS drop yesterday? Is this Performance Max campaign actually profitable against my Shopify revenue, or just against Google's reported number? Which of these five accounts needs me first this morning? Those are judgment questions, and neither a dashboard nor Performance Max is built to answer them. 

 The distinguishing traits are the ones platform AI structurally can't have: it works for the advertiser, not the auction; it reads across platforms as one account; it cross-checks platform-reported numbers against real revenue; and — the part that makes it safe on client money — it stays read-only by default, with a Trust Gate that flags any number it can't stand behind rather than acting on it. The judgment is automated. The last click stays yours. 

## How to choose

 Strip it to an if/then and the decision is quick: 

- If you just need to see the numbers and you're comfortable reading them yourself — a dashboard is enough. Don't over-buy.
 
- If you want the platform to auto-optimize within its own auction — bids, placements, volume at a target CPA or ROAS inside Google or Meta — reach for Performance Max (or Advantage+). Feed it clean data and it earns its keep.
 
- If you want judgment on your side of the table — reasoning across platforms, honest about what it can't measure, with approval-gated execution — you want an AI media buyer . It's the only one of the three that decides whether the auction-side tools are doing the right thing.

 And notice these aren't mutually exclusive. The common, correct answer for a serious advertiser is: run Performance Max where it earns, keep a dashboard for a quick glance, and put an AI media buyer over the top to supervise both — to catch the Performance Max campaign that's winning on paper and losing against real revenue. 

## Where Adgent sits

 Adgent is the AI media buyer in that stack. It runs one loop — Aware · Ask · Apply . Overnight it reads every account across Meta, Google and TikTok and writes the verdict first: a brief ranked by impact, not a wall of charts. You ask it "why did Meta ROAS drop?" and get a strategist's answer with evidence. When a fix is ready, it's a prepared action you approve with one message — read-only by default, every write approval-gated. Its Trust Gate refuses to stand behind a number it can't verify against real revenue, which is exactly the check Performance Max's own reporting can't give you. 

 If you want the longer definition, read what an AI media buyer actually is , or see the reasoning in action in how to audit a Google Ads account . When you're ready to see what it says about a real account — including your Performance Max campaigns — request a demo : fifteen minutes, connected read-only. 

## Frequently asked

Q: Is Performance Max an AI media buyer? 
 No. Performance Max is Google's own AI, optimizing bids and placements toward Google's auction. An AI media buyer sits on the advertiser's side, reasons across Meta, Google and TikTok together, cross-checks against real revenue, and executes only on your approval. Performance Max is a campaign type; an AI media buyer is a decision-maker that works for you. 

Q: Can an AI media buyer replace Performance Max? 
 No, and it isn't meant to. Performance Max runs the auction-side bidding inside Google Ads; an AI media buyer sits a layer above and decides whether Performance Max is the right campaign to run, how much budget it should get, and whether its reported ROAS holds up against real revenue. They operate at different layers, so the AI media buyer manages Performance Max rather than replacing it. 

Q: Do I need both Performance Max and an AI media buyer? 
 Often yes. Performance Max is a strong auction-side tool when you feed it good signals and real conversion data. An AI media buyer is what decides whether those signals are right, whether the campaign is actually profitable against your store revenue, and what to change when it drifts. One executes bids; the other holds the judgment. 

Q: Is a dashboard enough for a small account? 
 Sometimes. If you run a single channel, check it daily yourself, and already know what each metric means, a dashboard that renders your data cleanly can be enough. The moment you're across two or more platforms, or you find yourself asking why a number moved and not just what it is, a dashboard stops answering the question you actually have. 

Q: Which is best for an agency? 
 An AI media buyer, in most cases. Agencies run many accounts across Meta, Google and TikTok and can't read every one deeply every morning. A read-only-by-default AI media buyer that reasons across all of them, flags untrustworthy numbers, and prepares approval-gated changes gives leverage no dashboard or single-platform automation can. Performance Max still runs inside the Google accounts; the AI media buyer supervises it. 

Q: Does an AI media buyer work with Performance Max campaigns? 
 Yes. A good AI media buyer reads your Performance Max campaigns like any other, cross-checks their platform-reported ROAS against real revenue, flags when the reported numbers can't be trusted yet, and prepares budget or structure changes for your approval. It works with Performance Max, not against it. 

Q: What's the difference between platform AI and an AI media buyer? 
 Platform AI — Performance Max, Meta Advantage+ — optimizes toward the platform's own auction and hides account structure from the buyer. An AI media buyer works on the advertiser's side, reasons across platforms as one account, cross-checks platform numbers against real revenue, explains why a metric moved, and executes only on your approval. Same word AI, opposite side of the table.

# How to audit a Google Ads account, properly

 Most account audits are a 40-point checklist that produces a 40-point to-do list. This one is shorter and ordered differently: the checks that move money first, ranked by recoverable spend — not by how thorough the report looks. 

 N 
 
 Nurettin Demiral 
 Notes from building the analyst 

 Short answer 
 To audit a Google Ads account, work in order of recoverable spend: first verify conversion tracking (a broken signal corrupts everything downstream), then check wasted search terms , placement leakage , bid-strategy fit and budget pacing . Attach a dollar estimate to each finding and fix the highest-value leak first — a useful audit ranks by impact, not by how many boxes it ticks. 

 A Google Ads account audit is supposed to answer one question: where is this account leaking money, and what's the cheapest thing to fix first? Yet most audits bury that answer under a hundred green ticks. So here's the version that leads with impact — the checks that actually recover spend, in the order a senior buyer runs them. 

## Start with the number you can't trust

 Before you judge a single campaign, judge the measurement. An account optimizing toward a mis-configured or value-less conversion will make every downstream number lie — and no amount of bid tuning fixes a broken signal. So the first check isn't performance, it's whether performance is even measurable. 

 An audit that trusts a broken number just optimizes the account deeper into the wrong thing. 

 This is the same discipline behind leading with the verdict : say what you can stand behind, flag what you can't, and never manufacture confidence. 

## The 12-point audit, ranked by recoverable spend

- Conversion tracking integrity. Are conversions firing once, with the right value, on the right action? Duplicate or value-less conversions corrupt every optimization downstream. Verify against Google's conversion tracking setup .
 
- Wasted search terms. Pull the search terms report and find spend on queries that will never convert. This is usually the single largest pool of instantly recoverable budget.
 
- Placement leakage. On Display, Video and Performance Max, spend drifts to low-quality placements at a fraction of core performance — the Google-side twin of the placement drift that hides inside a blended average .
 
- Bid strategy fit. Is the strategy matched to the data volume? Target CPA/ROAS need enough conversions to learn; below that threshold they thrash.
 
- Budget pacing. Which campaigns are capped while winners starve? Reallocation is free performance.
 
- Search vs Performance Max overlap. PMax can cannibalize your branded and high-intent search. Check where they compete for the same query.
 
- Negative keyword hygiene. Missing negatives let broad match bleed into irrelevant queries — this compounds with check 2.
 
- Audience and geo signal. Are you feeding the algorithm useful audience signals, or letting it guess?
 
- Ad strength and asset coverage. Thin responsive assets cap reach and quality — but chase this after the money checks, not before.
 
- Landing page and tracking parity. Do the pages match the promise, and does analytics agree with Ads on conversions?
 
- Attribution maturity. Recent numbers are still settling. A campaign judged inside its window reads pessimistic — the Google cousin of Meta's 7-day window problem .
 
- Cross-platform comparability. If you also run Meta, remember the two can't be compared directly before you shift budget between them.

## Rank, don't just list

 The difference between a useful audit and a report nobody acts on is ordering. Every finding should carry an estimate of recoverable spend so the account owner knows what to do Monday morning — not a flat list where a $12,000/month leak sits next to a cosmetic asset warning. 

- Attach a number to each finding. "Wasted search terms: ~$9,000/mo" beats "improve keyword targeting."
 
- Sequence the fixes. Some changes reset learning; order them so you don't undo one fix with the next.
 
- Make every figure verifiable. Each number should link back to where to confirm it in the account. Trust is the whole point.

## Why this doesn't stay done

 The catch with a one-off audit is that accounts drift. Search terms shift weekly, placements re-leak, pacing changes with seasonality. A PDF from last quarter describes an account that no longer exists. The real fix isn't a better audit template — it's a standing analyst that re-audits continuously and flags what changed. 

 That's what Adgent does for Google, Meta and TikTok as one account: it runs these checks every morning, ranks findings by recoverable spend, and prepares the fix for your approval. If you want to see it against a real account, request a demo — fifteen minutes, connected read-only. 

## Red flags: what a broken account looks like

 Some findings you have to calculate. Others you can spot in thirty seconds — the tells that an account has been left on autopilot. If you see any of these, you've found recoverable spend before you've done a single sum. 

- Conversion action set to "Every" instead of "One." A lead form counting every submission inflates your conversion count and teaches Smart Bidding to chase repeat form-fillers. For lead gen, this single toggle can overstate conversions by 20–40%.
 
- No audience exclusions on retargeting or PMax. You're paying full CPA to re-serve ads to people who already converted. On accounts with a healthy return rate, that's often 5–15% of spend re-buying customers you already own.
 
- Performance Max with zero audience signals. An empty signal means the algorithm cold-starts on your money. PMax will find the cheapest conversions it can — usually your existing branded traffic — and take credit for demand you'd have captured for free.
 
- A search terms report nobody has opened in 90 days. Broad match without weekly negative-keyword pruning bleeds into junk queries continuously. If the last negative was added six months ago, assume 10–30% of search spend is landing on queries that will never convert.
 
- Target CPA/ROAS running on under 15 conversions in 30 days. Below roughly 30 conversions a month per campaign, Smart Bidding doesn't have enough signal to learn — it thrashes, and CPA swings wildly week to week.
 
- One "catch-all" ad group with 50+ keywords. Structure this loose means no query gets a relevant ad or landing page. Quality Score suffers, CPCs climb, and you can't diagnose anything because everything is averaged together.
 
- Auto-applied recommendations turned on. Google's auto-apply will quietly expand match types and raise budgets on its own schedule. An account where Google is making the changes isn't an account you're auditing — it's one you're discovering.
 
- Display and Search fused in one campaign. "Search with Display Select" pools two totally different funnels under one budget and one CPA target. The Display half almost always drags, hidden inside the blended number — the same trap as placement drift inside an average .
 
- Ads-reported conversions that don't match analytics. When Google Ads claims 400 conversions and GA4 sees 250, someone is optimizing on a number that isn't real. A gap wider than about 15–20% means your measurement is the first thing to fix, not your bids.

## Manual audit vs. continuous audit

 The format of the audit decides what it can actually catch. A human running a checklist once a quarter and software reading the account through the API every morning are looking for the same leaks — but they see very different amounts of them. 

 Manual audit 
 Automated / continuous audit 

 Time to run 
 A few hours to a full day per account 
 Minutes — and it re-runs on its own 

 Frequency 
 Quarterly, if you're disciplined 
 Every day 

 Coverage 
 What the auditor has time to sample 
 Every campaign, term and placement 

 Catches drift 
 No — it's a snapshot that ages fast 
 Yes — flags what changed since yesterday 

 Best at 
 Judgement calls, strategy, context 
 High-drift leaks: search terms, placements, pacing 

 Cost 
 $500–$3,000 for a one-off agency audit 
 Software fee, then near-zero per re-run 

 Neither wins outright. A manual audit brings judgement a script can't; a continuous one catches the leaks that reopen the week after a manual audit ships. The strongest setup is a standing analyst for the high-drift checks and a human for the strategy calls — which is exactly what an AI media buyer is built to be. 

## How often should you audit?

 Audit cadence should match how fast each check goes stale. Some things barely move; others rot within a week. A single quarterly review treats them all the same, which is why so many audits feel out of date the moment they land. 

- Full audit — quarterly. The complete 12-point pass: structure, bid strategy, attribution, account hygiene. These are slow-moving and worth a deep, unhurried look four times a year.
 
- Light review — monthly. A focused pass on budget pacing, ad strength and the accounts that shifted most. Thirty minutes to catch anything the quarter would let drift too far.
 
- Continuous — for the high-drift checks. Wasted search terms, placement leakage and budget pacing move week to week. These want monitoring, not scheduling — the moment they slip, spend leaks, and a quarterly cadence means you find out 89 days late.

 The blunt version: audit the slow things on a calendar and watch the fast things continuously. Anything you only check quarterly, you're effectively not checking. 

## Common Google Ads audit mistakes

 A bad audit isn't just an incomplete one — it's one that draws confident conclusions from bad inputs. These are the mistakes that turn a review into a list of wrong decisions. 

- Auditing before you've checked conversion tracking. Every performance judgement rests on the conversion signal. If you rank campaigns before verifying it fires once, with the right value, you're ranking noise. Measurement is step one for a reason.
 
- Treating small-sample placements as decisions. A placement with 40 impressions and one conversion isn't a winner, and one with 200 clicks and no sale isn't dead — it's just noise. Cutting or scaling on samples this thin creates the very volatility you were trying to fix.
 
- Ignoring the attribution window. Recent conversions are still landing. Judge a campaign from inside its window and it always reads pessimistic — the Google cousin of Meta's day-one data problem . Let the window close before you call a verdict.
 
- Comparing across platforms as if they measure the same thing. Google and Meta count conversions differently, so a raw ROAS comparison is apples to oranges. Reconcile the models before you move budget between them.
 
- Producing a to-do list instead of a ranking. A flat 40-point list where a $12,000/mo leak sits beside a cosmetic asset warning gets skimmed, not acted on. Rank by recoverable spend, or the audit changes nothing.

## Frequently asked

Q: What is a Google Ads account audit? 
 A structured review of how an account is built and spending — conversion tracking, search terms, placements, bid strategy, budget pacing and measurement — to find wasted spend and missed opportunity. A good audit ranks findings by recoverable spend, not by how many boxes it ticks. 

Q: How often should you audit a Google Ads account? 
 A full audit quarterly, a lighter review monthly. But the highest-value checks drift week to week, so the ideal is continuous monitoring rather than a one-off review. 

Q: How long does a Google Ads audit take? 
 A manual audit of a mid-sized account takes a senior buyer a few hours to a day. Software that reads the account through the API can surface the same findings in minutes and keep them current. 

Q: Can an AI audit a Google Ads account? 
 Yes. An AI ad account audit reads the account through the platform API, checks the same signals a senior buyer would, and ranks findings by recoverable spend — while flagging any number it can't yet stand behind. It should stay read-only and propose fixes for approval rather than changing the account on its own. 

Q: How long does a PPC audit take? 
 For a mid-sized Google Ads account, a thorough manual PPC audit runs a few hours to a full day — longer if the account is large or the tracking is messy. Agencies often quote a one-to-two-week turnaround because the report has to be written up. Software that reads the account through the API surfaces the same findings in minutes and, unlike a document, keeps them current. 

Q: What tools do you need to audit Google Ads? 
 The account itself gets you most of the way: the search terms report, the placements and asset reports, the change history, and the conversions and attribution settings. Pair it with GA4 to reconcile conversions, and Google Tag Assistant or the Chrome Tag Assistant to confirm tags fire correctly. Beyond that, a spreadsheet to rank findings by recoverable spend — or a continuous tool that does the reading and ranking for you. 

Q: How much can an audit recover? 
 It depends entirely on how neglected the account is, but on accounts that haven't been pruned in a while, 10–30% of spend is commonly sitting in wasted search terms, leaked placements and duplicate retargeting. The bigger, quieter win is measurement: fixing a mis-configured conversion often reshapes every downstream decision, which is worth more than any single line-item cut. 

Q: Can you automate a Google Ads audit? 
 The high-drift checks — wasted search terms, placement leakage, budget pacing — automate well, because they're rule-based and change constantly, so continuous monitoring beats a scheduled review. Judgement calls like strategy and structure still benefit from a human. The best setup automates the leaks that reopen weekly and keeps a person on the calls that need context.

 Playbook · 8 min read · July 2026 
 
# Why Performance Max isn’t spending your full budget

 You set the budget, the campaign is live, and it's spending a fraction of the cap. Before you touch the budget slider — it's probably not the budget. Here's how to diagnose the real constraint, ranked by how often it's the culprit. 

 N 
 
 Nurettin Demiral 
 Notes from building the analyst 

 Short answer 
 Most of the time it isn't a budget problem. In 2026 data, ~60% of "not spending" cases trace to a bid target or Quality Score constraint choking delivery, and only ~18% are genuinely budget-limited . Check in order: bid targets too aggressive, learning phase, asset approvals, thin signals, shared-budget cannibalization, then broken conversion tracking. Loosen the target before you raise the budget. 

 "Performance Max isn't spending my budget" is one of the most common panics in a Google Ads account — and one of the most misdiagnosed. The instinct is to raise the budget, or to assume the campaign is broken. Both are usually wrong. A PMax campaign that under-delivers is almost always telling you something specific about its bid targets, its learning state, or its inputs — and the fix depends entirely on which one. 

 So treat this as a diagnostic, not a checklist to blast through. Work the causes in order of likelihood, confirm each one before you act, and change one thing at a time. Here's the ranked list. 

 A campaign that won't spend is rarely short of budget. It's short of auctions it's allowed to win. 

## First: is it actually a budget problem?

 Before anything else, read the campaign status. Google labels a campaign "Limited by budget" only when it genuinely wants to spend more than the cap allows. If your status reads eligible , limited by bid strategy , or shows a learning notice, the budget is not your bottleneck — and raising it will do nothing except sit unspent. 

 This is the whole point of the headline stat. Across 2026 account audits, roughly 60% of "PMax isn't spending" cases resolved to a bid or Quality Score constraint , and only about 18% were truly budget-limited . The rest were split across approvals, tracking, and thin inputs. If you skip the status check and go straight to the budget, you're fixing the wrong four-in-five campaigns. 

## 1. Bid targets too aggressive (the most common cause)

 This is the one to check first, because it accounts for the largest slice. A tight Target ROAS or Target CPA tells Smart Bidding to only enter auctions it can win profitably at that target . Set the target too high — or too low a CPA — and the algorithm walks away from most of the auction. The result looks exactly like a budget problem: spend flatlines well under the cap. It isn't. The campaign is behaving as instructed; you instructed it to be picky. 

 The fix is counterintuitive: loosen or remove the target and let it learn . Start with "Maximize conversions" (or "Maximize conversion value") with no target, let the campaign gather data and find volume, then introduce a target gradually once you can see what CPA or ROAS it actually achieves. Setting an aggressive target on day one is asking the system to optimize against a number it has no data to hit yet. 

- Status reads "limited by bid strategy" or eligible, not "limited by budget."
 
- Your Target CPA sits below your recent historical CPA, or your Target ROAS above recent achieved ROAS.
 
- Fix: drop the target, run Maximize conversions, reintroduce the target after 2–3 weeks of data.

## 2. You're still in the learning phase

 For the first 7–14 days after launch — or after any significant edit — PMax delivers slowly and conservatively while Smart Bidding calibrates. Under-spend during this window is expected, not broken. The trap is impatience: you tweak the budget, then the target, then the assets, and each edit resets the learning clock , so the campaign never gets a clean run to ramp. 

 The discipline here is the same one that keeps a winning campaign winning: batch your changes, then leave it alone. If you're mid-learning, the right move is often to do nothing for a week. We wrote the full version of this in how to scale a winner without breaking it — the mechanics of editing without resetting learning apply directly to a campaign that's still trying to find its feet. 

## 3. Ad review delays and disapproved assets

 New asset groups go through review, which typically takes up to 24–48 hours and can run longer. Until assets are approved, they can't serve — so a brand-new campaign that isn't spending may simply be waiting. Worse is the silent case: some assets are disapproved (policy, trademark, low-quality landing page) while others are approved, so the campaign serves in a starved, partial state and never reaches the channels the disapproved assets would have unlocked. 

 Check the asset group status and the policy column. A single disapproved video or a rejected final URL can quietly cap delivery on YouTube or Display while Search chugs along, making the total look like a mysterious under-spend. 

## 4. Narrow audience signals or thin asset groups

 PMax uses your audience signals as a starting hint and your assets as the raw material to assemble ads for every channel. Give it too little of either and it has nothing to work with. A thin asset group — one headline variant, no video, a single image — can't build a YouTube or Display ad at all, so those channels stay dark and delivery concentrates on the one or two it can serve. Similarly, a narrow or empty audience signal gives Smart Bidding a weak cold-start. 

- Fill every asset slot: multiple headlines, descriptions, images, and at least one video (Google will auto-generate a poor one if you don't — supply your own).
 
- Add strong audience signals — your customer lists, high-intent custom segments — so the system has a warm start, not a cold one.

## 5. Shared-budget cannibalization with Search

 If PMax and your Search campaigns sit in a shared budget pool , PMax can starve Search — or the reverse can mask what's really happening. PMax has broader auction access than a standard Search campaign: it can serve across Search, Shopping, YouTube, Display, Gmail and Discover, so in a shared pool it often out-competes Search for the same money. The pool drains, and it looks like one campaign "won't spend" when really another is eating the budget. 

 The fix is structural: give PMax and Search their own separate budgets so neither can cannibalize the other. This is the flip side of a problem we covered in depth — when PMax quietly absorbs your brand traffic and inflates its own numbers at Search's expense. If that's your situation, read Performance Max brand cannibalization for how to detect and fence it off. 

## 6. Broken conversion tracking

 This one is last by frequency but first by damage. Smart Bidding spends toward conversions it can measure . If your conversion tag is broken, double-firing, mapped to the wrong conversion action, or reporting zero, the algorithm has no signal to optimize against — so it does the safe thing and pulls delivery back. From the outside it looks like a budget or bid problem; underneath, the campaign is flying blind. 

 Before you conclude anything else, confirm conversions are firing cleanly and landing on the right action. If you're not sure how to check end to end, our Google Ads account audit walkthrough covers the tracking checks that catch this — a mis-configured conversion event is one of the most common reasons a healthy-looking campaign refuses to spend. 

## The diagnostic tool: the Channel Performance report

 You don't have to guess which of these it is. Open the campaign, go to Insights , and read the Channel Performance report . It breaks delivery down by Search, Shopping, Display, YouTube, Gmail and Discover — so you can see, directly, which channel is silent. 

 That silence is the tell. A dark YouTube row usually means a missing video asset or a disapproval. A dark Shopping row points at feed or Merchant Center issues. Broad silence across everything but Search points back at the bid target or the learning phase. The report turns "it won't spend" from a mystery into a specific, addressable gap. 

## Work it in order — and change one thing at a time

 The mistake that keeps campaigns stuck isn't missing a cause; it's fixing three at once, resetting learning, and losing the ability to tell what worked. Read the status. Check the target. Give it time. Confirm approvals and tracking. Read the Channel Performance report. Change one variable, then wait for the campaign to respond before you touch the next. 

 This is exactly the kind of ranked, evidence-first diagnosis an AI media buyer is built to do. Instead of you eyeballing six possible causes, it reads the account, identifies which constraint is actually binding, ranks the fix by impact, and shows its work — the status flag, the target gap, the silent channel. If you want that run against a real PMax campaign, our Performance Max audit guide shows what a full read looks like, or you can request a demo and see what it says about yours — fifteen minutes, connected read-only. 

## Frequently asked

Q: Why is Performance Max not spending my full budget? 
 Usually it isn't a budget problem at all. In 2026 data, ~60% of "not spending" cases trace to a bid target or Quality Score constraint choking delivery, and only ~18% are genuinely budget-limited. Check bid targets, learning phase and asset approvals before touching the budget. 

Q: Is it a budget problem or a bid problem? 
 Almost always a bid problem. The status will say "Limited by budget" only when the campaign truly wants more spend. If it reads eligible or limited by bid strategy, a tight Target ROAS or CPA is the real cap — loosen the target before you raise the budget. 

Q: Does a tight tCPA or tROAS limit spend? 
 Yes — this is the single most common cause. An aggressive Target CPA or ROAS tells Smart Bidding to only enter auctions it can win profitably, so it walks away from most of them. Start with no target, let it learn, then tighten gradually. 

Q: How long is the PMax learning phase? 
 Roughly 7–14 days after launch or a significant edit. During it, delivery ramps slowly and is deliberately conservative. Any major change — budget, target, assets, or audience signals — resets the clock, so batch your edits and then leave it alone. 

Q: Does Performance Max steal budget from Search? 
 In a shared budget pool, yes. PMax has broader auction access than a standard Search campaign, so it outcompetes Search for the same pool and can starve it. Give PMax and Search separate budgets so one campaign's delivery can't cannibalize the other. 

Q: How do I see where PMax is spending? 
 Open the campaign, go to Insights, and read the Channel Performance report. It breaks delivery down by Search, Shopping, Display, YouTube, Gmail and Discover, so you can see which channel is silent and whether an asset gap or signal gap is the reason. 

Q: Can broken conversion tracking stop PMax spending? 
 Yes. Smart Bidding spends toward conversions it can measure. If your conversion tag is broken, double-firing, or reporting into the wrong action, the algorithm can't find signal to optimize against and pulls back delivery. Audit tracking before you blame the budget. 

 Keep reading 
 
 Playbook How to audit a Performance Max campaign 
 Playbook Performance Max brand cannibalization

URL: https://adgent.app/google-ads-account-structure-2026
========================================================================
Adgent 

 EN TR

 Product 
 Pricing 
 About 
 Blog 
 EN TR 
 Request demo 

 Playbook · 9 min read · July 2026 
 
# Google Ads account structure in 2026 (and how to audit yours)

 The old rules of structure were about manual control. In 2026 they're about signal quality — building an account so Smart Bidding has enough clean conversion data to actually learn. Here's the modern default, and how to audit whether yours measures up. 

 N 
 
 Nurettin Demiral 
 Notes from building the analyst 

 Short answer 
 In 2026, Google Ads structure is about signal quality, not manual control — give Smart Bidding enough conversions per unit to learn. The modern default: Single Theme Ad Groups (STAGs) of 3-10 tight keywords, a Brand / Core Non-brand / (optional) Competitor campaign split, and 30+ conversions per month per campaign so bidding has signal. Structure to feed the algorithm, not to fight it. 

 For fifteen years, account structure was how you controlled Google Ads by hand. You split campaigns to control budgets, split ad groups to control bids, and the buyer with the most granular structure usually won. That advice is now actively wrong. Smart Bidding sets the bids; your structure's only job is to feed it clean, concentrated signal. Build for that and you win. Build the old way — a hundred single-keyword ad groups, a campaign per product — and you starve the algorithm across so many thin units that it never learns. 

 So this is the 2026 default, framed the way we think about it at Adgent: not just how to build the structure, but how to audit whether yours is right. Because most accounts we read weren't built wrong on purpose — they were built correct for 2018 and never re-cut for how bidding works now. 

## The hierarchy, and what each level is for now

 The four levels haven't changed. What each one is for has changed completely: 

- Account. The billing and conversion-tracking boundary. One conversion action set, one source of truth. This is where signal is defined — if your conversion tracking is wrong here, nothing below it can be right.
 
- Campaign. The bidding unit. This is where budget lives and where Smart Bidding learns. The campaign is now the thing you must feed with enough conversions — it's the level the algorithm optimizes against.
 
- Ad group. The relevance unit. Its only remaining job is keeping keywords, ads and landing page tightly matched — it no longer controls bids at all. This is where STAGs live.
 
- Ads & keywords. The message and the trigger. Keywords tell Google what intent you want; responsive search ads answer it. Quality Score still rides on how well the two align.

 The shift in one line: campaigns are for bidding, ad groups are for relevance. Every structural mistake in 2026 is really a confusion of those two jobs — either splitting bidding units so fine they can't learn, or lumping unrelated intents into one ad group so no ad can answer them. 

## STAGs: the one rule that governs ad groups

 A Single Theme Ad Group is exactly what it says: an ad group where every keyword shares one intent. Three to ten keywords, all so closely related that a single ad and a single landing page can answer every one of them. That's not a style preference — it's the mechanism that drives relevance and Quality Score, and Google itself reports that raising ad relevance and landing-page experience is one of the largest levers on Quality Score , which in turn discounts your cost per click. 

 The rule is a single test, and it's brutal in how it decides: 

 Every keyword in an ad group must be answerable by the same ad and the same landing page. If you can't write one ad for all of them, the group is too broad — split it. 

 Take "running shoes." If your ad group holds running shoes , trail running shoes , marathon shoes and waterproof running shoes , ask: can one headline honestly answer all four? It can't. Someone searching "waterproof running shoes" who lands on a generic running-shoe page bounces, and their bounce trains Smart Bidding that the whole group converts worse than it should. Split them into four STAGs, each with its own ad and its own page, and every impression now carries a clean signal. 

 The 2018 version of this advice was SKAGs — Single Keyword Ad Groups, one keyword each. Close-variant matching killed that: Google now serves your ad on dozens of variants regardless, so a one-keyword group buys you nothing but fragmentation. The STAG is the correction — tight enough for relevance, broad enough that conversions concentrate instead of scattering across a hundred starved groups. 

## The campaign split: isolate brand, above all

 At the campaign level, split by intent , not by product line or geography or anything else that fragments signal. The modern default is three buckets: 

- Brand. People searching your name. High intent, high conversion rate, low CPA — a different economy entirely.
 
- Core Non-brand. Your real prospecting — category and problem-aware searches where the money is actually won or lost.
 
- Competitor (optional). Bids on rival names. Expensive, low conversion rate — worth isolating so its poor efficiency never contaminates non-brand targets.

 The non-negotiable one is brand . Brand searches convert at rates that dwarf non-brand — often several times higher at a fraction of the CPA — because the person already decided to buy from you. Leave brand mixed into non-brand and those cheap conversions flatter the blended average, hiding the fact that your actual prospecting is losing money. Worse, a single target CPA can't serve both economies at once: set it for brand and non-brand starves; set it for non-brand and you overpay for traffic you'd have won for free. Isolating brand is the single highest-leverage structural move in most accounts — the full case for it, including how Performance Max quietly eats brand credit, is in why brand must be isolated from Performance Max . 

## The signal threshold: 30+ conversions a month, or bidding thrashes

 Here's the number that quietly governs everything above. Google's own guidance is that Target CPA wants roughly 30 conversions in the trailing 30 days per campaign before its model is stable, and Target ROAS wants closer to 50 . Below that, Smart Bidding is fitting a curve to noise — it over-reacts to a lucky week, pulls back after a random dead Tuesday, and never settles. That's the thrash: bids swinging not because the market moved, but because the algorithm can't tell the difference between signal and randomness on too few data points. 

 This is why granular structure now backfires. Split one healthy campaign carrying 90 conversions/month into six tidy product campaigns and you've made six units of 15 — all below threshold, all thrashing, all worse than the one you started with. The instinct to organize neatly is exactly the instinct to resist. Consolidate until each campaign clears the threshold, then stop. If you're weighing how to group budgets for this, the mechanics are in CBO vs ABO in 2026 ; if you're trying to grow a campaign that's already learning without resetting it, see how to scale a winner without breaking it . 

## Structure for signal, not control — the 2026 shift

 Everything above collapses into one mental model, and it's the whole point of this piece. The old question was "how do I structure this so I can control every bid?" The new question is "how do I structure this so the algorithm has the cleanest, most concentrated signal to learn from?" 

 Those two questions produce opposite accounts. Controlling by hand pushes you toward more units, finer splits, single-keyword groups — maximum granularity. Feeding signal pushes you toward fewer, denser units — enough separation to keep intents clean, enough consolidation to keep every bidding unit above threshold. In 2026 the second account wins every time, because you are no longer the bidder. You're the person who decides what the bidder gets to see. 

 You don't structure a Google Ads account to control the bids anymore. You structure it to control what the algorithm can learn. 

## How to audit your existing structure

 Now the part most guides skip. You almost certainly already have an account — the question isn't how to build one from scratch, it's whether the one you've got is quietly leaking signal. Run these four checks, in order of how much damage they do: 

### 1. Are any ad groups too broad?

 Open your biggest ad groups and apply the STAG test to each: could one ad honestly answer every keyword in here? If the group mixes intents you couldn't write a single relevant headline for, it's too broad — every mismatched impression is teaching Smart Bidding the wrong thing. This is the most common leak and the easiest to fix. 

### 2. Are any campaigns below the conversion threshold?

 Sort campaigns by conversions over the last 30 days. Anything under ~30 is a thrashing bidding unit, full stop. It either needs consolidating into a sibling or its bid strategy needs to move up to a portfolio that pools signal across campaigns. Thin campaigns don't just underperform — they degrade. 

### 3. Is brand mixed into non-brand?

 Check whether brand terms live in the same campaign as prospecting. If they do, your reported CPA is a fiction — cheap brand conversions are subsidizing losing non-brand targets, and you can't see it. Pull brand into its own campaign and watch the non-brand numbers tell the truth, often for the first time. 

### 4. Is Performance Max overlapping Search?

 If you run both, PMax will happily serve on queries your Search campaigns should own — including brand — and take the credit, because it has priority on many query types. That overlap makes both structures unreadable. Two of our deeper walkthroughs cover the full sweep: how to audit a Google Ads account end-to-end, and how to audit Performance Max specifically for cannibalization. 

 Structural check The old rule (manual control) The 2026 rule (signal quality) 

 Keywords per ad group 1 (SKAG) 3-10, single theme (STAG) 
 Campaigns per account Many, granular Few, above threshold 
 Split campaigns by Product / geo Intent (brand / non-brand) 
 Bids set by You, by hand Smart Bidding 
 Conversions per campaign Not a concern 30+ / month (50 for tROAS) 

 Same four levels, opposite instincts. In 2026, granularity is the failure mode. 

## The close: let something read the whole structure at once

 The hard part of a structure audit isn't any single check — it's holding all four in your head at once, across every campaign, and seeing how they interact. A broad ad group inside a thin campaign that's also being cannibalized by PMax is three problems compounding, and a human scanning the UI at 8am rarely catches the compound. 

 That's exactly the read an AI media buyer is built for: it ingests the whole account structure at once, flags the ad groups too broad to answer with one ad, the campaigns below threshold, the brand bleed and the PMax overlap — ranked by how much each is costing you, with the fix prepared and waiting for your approval. If you want to see what it would say about your structure, request a demo — fifteen minutes, connected read-only. 

## Frequently asked

Q: What's the best Google Ads account structure in 2026? 
 Single Theme Ad Groups of 3-10 tightly related keywords, grouped into a small number of campaigns split by intent — Brand, Core Non-brand, and optionally Competitor — with each campaign carrying 30+ conversions per month so Smart Bidding has enough signal to learn. Structure for signal, not manual control. 

Q: What is a Single Theme Ad Group (STAG)? 
 A STAG is an ad group where every keyword shares one intent, so a single ad and a single landing page can answer all of them. The test: if you can't write one relevant ad for the whole group, it's too broad and needs splitting. 

Q: How many ad groups per campaign? 
 There's no fixed number — enough to keep each ad group a single tight theme, few enough that the campaign still clears the conversion threshold. In practice that's a handful to a couple of dozen STAGs, consolidated so budget and signal aren't spread too thin. 

Q: Should brand be a separate campaign? 
 Yes. Brand traffic converts at a far higher rate and lower CPA than non-brand, so mixing them lets cheap brand conversions flatter the average and hide non-brand losses. Isolating brand keeps its efficiency out of your prospecting numbers and stops Smart Bidding from over-crediting it. 

Q: How many conversions does Smart Bidding need? 
 Google's guidance is roughly 30 conversions in the past 30 days per campaign for tCPA, and closer to 50 for tROAS, before Smart Bidding has stable signal. Below that the algorithm thrashes — chasing noise — so consolidate campaigns to clear the threshold. 

Q: How do I audit my Google Ads account structure? 
 Check four things: are any ad groups too broad to answer with one ad? Are any campaigns below ~30 conversions/month? Is brand mixed into non-brand? Is Performance Max overlapping and cannibalizing Search? Each is a structural leak that quietly starves Smart Bidding of clean signal. 

Q: Does account structure still matter with AI bidding? 
 More than ever — it just changed jobs. You no longer structure to control bids by hand; you structure to feed the algorithm clean, concentrated conversion signal. Bad structure doesn't just waste effort now, it actively degrades what Smart Bidding can learn. 

 Keep reading

 Playbook How to audit a Google Ads account
